zwt49epsdedw__d16dffbae9d86a73cb343506e6.text:
[Summary]
Ace Alliance finds the proposed "2025 Net Change Limit Extension" Info Action Constitutional, subject to meeting the required DRep voting threshold of \>50% of the active voting stake when the Action expires at epoch boundary 603-604.

[Rationale Statement]
A [PDF version][pdf-link] of this rationale is also made available.

[pdf-link]: https://ipfs.io/ipfs/QmWkQwEdRNqunkFXmedoFZCubvZbm2zxuU3Yv1hokZdRBC

If approved, this Net Change Limit (NCL) type Info Action (`gov_action169kllwhfmp488je5x5rwvufd08p8sztdcf0ghf5sp6ey2gnjdwaqql47xry`) would extend the duration of  the previously approved 2025 NCL (`gov_action1nd3t833j7v5sz65k3tp9yyvztw60sjcjgcgjr37682s3m7frwrusqmd2k80`)by eight (8) epochs, concluding at the end of Epoch 612, without changing the total authorized amount of 350 million ada.

We find that this Action satisfies the applicable requirements of the Cardano Constitution.

**Constitutional Alignment** 

This governance action::

* **Satisfies the procedural requirements of Article III, Section 5:** The action is presented in a standardized, legible format including a URL and hash for off-chain materials. It provides sufficient rationale for the extension, satisfying the transparency requirements of the Constitution.  
* **Is the appropriate mechanism for changing the NCL state (Article III, Section 4):** Because the Net Change Limit is currently enforced through Constitutional Committee oversight rather than engrained protocol parameters, an Info Action is the correct mechanism for this adjustment. This Action creates an on-chain record of the community's intent to extend the NCL duration, applied and enforced by the Constitutional Committee through its evaluation of pertinent Treasury Withdrawal Governance Actions.  
* **Adheres to Guardrail TREASURY-01a:** The Constitution mandates that "A net change limit for the Cardano treasury's balance per period of time must be agreed by the DReps via an on-chain governance action with a threshold of greater than 50% of the active voting stake". Because the "period of time" is a defining parameter of any Net Change Limit, modifying the expiration date requires the explicit agreement of the DReps. This Info Action serves as the constitutionally prescribed mechanism to solicit and secure such agreement on-chain.

[Precedent Discussion]
This action follows the precedent of the original "2025 Net Change Limit" governance action, utilizing the Info Action to define the parameters of the limit. We find this to be consistent with the current technical implementation of Treasury guardrails. A Net Change Limit may be set or changed at any time according to the Cardano Constitution, provided that it satisfies relevant constitutional requirements.

[Counterargument Discussion]
It might be asserted that an Info Action cannot establish an enforceable condition for subsequent adjudication. This hasty generalization improperly conflates Info Actions in general with the Constitution-enabled subset of Info Actions pertaining to the Net Change Limit. Because the Constitution (Appendix I, TREASURY-01a) explicitly requires the NCL to be agreed by DReps via an on-chain action, this Info Action is the constitutionally prescribed method for establishing that agreement.

However, we emphasize that the enforceability of this extension is strictly conditional. As required by Guardrail `TREASURY-01a`, this NCL extension will only be considered valid and binding for future assessments if it receives greater than 50% of the active voting stake of DReps at the time of its expiration. Without meeting this specific threshold, the extension cannot be validly enforced by the Constitutional Committee.

[Conclusion]
This Net Change Limit type Info Action meets the applicable constitutional requirements.

[Internal Vote]
{"constitutional":5,"unconstitutional":0,"abstain":0,"didNotVote":0,"againstVote":0}

zwt49epsdedw__8f54d021c6e6fcdd5a4908f10a.text:
[Summary]
We find the “Cardano Critical Integrations Budget” Info Action Unconstitutional while the CC size is below committeeMinSize parameter. This condition may be remedied before voting ends at the end of Epoch 603.

[Rationale Statement]
A [PDF version][pdf-link] of this rationale is also made available.

[pdf-link]: https://ipfs.io/ipfs/QmP8p2WzLwcVbwhkEaHniE4GGqek7U3APtYsDktLG3KtUt

The Ace Alliance is casting an initial No vote on this Budget Info Action as a procedural safeguard because the Constitutional Committee size is currently below the committeeMinSize parameter. This decision is not an assessment of its merits. Under Article VII, Section 4 of the Cardano Constitution, no governance action other than a Motion of no confidence or an Update Constitutional Committee may be implemented on-chain unless a requisite percentage of the Constitutional Committee first determines and affirms that it does not violate the Constitution. In the specific case of Info Actions that propose an ecosystem budget, Article VII, Section 4 further requires the Committee to record an on-chain opinion as to whether the proposed budget, if implemented as described, would violate the Constitution. Because ada holders have set a committeeMinSize of seven (7)  through governance parameters and Guardrails, this condition must be remedied before the Constitutional Committee can responsibly express a positive opinion on the constitutionality of this budget.

The existence of a minimum committee size parameter in the ledger instantiates the ideal that there must be a minimum number of Constitutional Committee members participating when decisions that affect the chain’s governance are made. In the Cardano Blockchain Guardrails Appendix, this is expressed as: “The minimum size of the Constitutional Committee (committeeMinSize) – the least number of members that can be included in a Constitutional Committee following a governance action to change the Constitutional Committee.”  The most recent “Update Committee” governance action featuring seven CC credentials was “Replace Interim Constitutional Committee” (gov\_action1g7sw0f8e8qa34lppj2erksvzf4j6e9udwaq6efslc8apdqeazygsq2spyyt).   The minimum number of Constitutional Committee members  is not only a technical constraint but also a social commitment. On the technical layer, the ledger enforces this ideal for actions that have an on-chain effect when enacted, such as parameter changes or treasury withdrawals, by preventing enactment if the configured minimum is not met. On the social layer, the members of the Constitutional Committee have a parallel responsibility to instantiate that  same principle in how they exercise their constitutional review function, including for Info Actions that relate to Cardano Treasury policy. In other words, the parameter is an expression of how the system is supposed to work; the ledger encodes and enforces the minimum size for enactment, and the Committee applies that same principle in its own assessment on constitutionality.

At the time of this initial vote, only six (6)  of the required seven (7) Constitutional Committee members are seated, and a nomination and election process for another seat is ongoing. Issuing a Yes opinion on a multi-epoch ecosystem budget while the Committee is operating below its configured minimum would call into question the  legitimacy of both the budget and any subsequent treasury withdrawals that rely on it. Ace Alliance therefore records a No at this stage. However, we are actively considering other aspects of this Action’s  constitutionality with the intention to update this vote and rationale.

[Precedent Discussion]
We find that, where an Info Action is reasonably expected to lead to substantive on-chain policy or spending, the Action must be reviewed and decided by a Constitutional Committee size satisfying the commiteeMinSize parameter. When an Info Action is used as a required precursor for future Cardano Treasury policy, as described in Article VII, Section 4, it is functionally part of the on-chain policy pipeline.

We do not conclude that satisfying commiteeMinSize is necessary for all Info Actions, particularly those that will not themselves serve as a foundation for future Cardano Treasury Policy (i.e. purely informational polls that gauge community sentiment, exploratory questions about direction, or other non-binding surveys of opinion). Such sentiment-focused Info Actions do not require the input of the Constitutional Committee, therefore a fully seated CC is not required for constitutional legitimacy.

[Counterargument Discussion]
Some would argue that because the Ledger does not prevent CC members from voting on Budget Info Actions, a fully seated Committee is not required for those votes to be valid. We reject this argument specifically for Budget Info Actions. These actions are explicitly designed to sit in the causal chain that leads to on-chain Treasury Withdrawals, so the CC’s opinion on them is not merely advisory but a prerequisite for subsequent spending. 

During the design of Voltaire governance, it was seriously considered that voting should be halted entirely when voting parameters such as committeMinSize were not satisfied. This idea was not adopted, opting instead to allow votes to be cast and stored so they could later be counted if and when all conditions were met. Because the Ledger cannot tell the difference between ordinary Info Actions, where CC votes may have no downstream effect, and Budget Info Actions, where they clearly do, it is up to the Constitutional Committee to uphold the ideal of the minimum committee size in this specific context.

[Conclusion]
In conclusion, we find that the number of active CC credentials must meet the commiteeMinSize parameter in order to consider the constitutionality of Budget Info Actions. This best upholds Article VII, Section 4 of the Cardano Constitution, which mandates that the Committee affirm the constitutionality of proposed budgets before they can be implemented on-chain.

The Ace Alliance will revisit and revise this vote once the number of active CC credentials reaches the committeeMinSize parameter.

[Internal Vote]
{"constitutional":0,"unconstitutional":5,"abstain":0,"didNotVote":0,"againstVote":0}

zwt49epsdedw__a36eafaea085b77f97cceacf07.text:
[Summary]
The Ace Alliance finds the proposed “Securing Generic Top-Level Domains for the Cardano Ecosystem” Info Action Constitutional. We comment only on its constitutionality, not the merits of the initiative itself, in line with Article VII, Section 4 of the Cardano Constitution.

[Rationale Statement]
A [PDF version][pdf-link] of this rationale is also made available.

[pdf-link]: https://ipfs.io/ipfs/QmeKeNSfteJvuF3C6pVeCVi7rvSF9j9oJ7mu7xHALJSgSa

The Ace Alliance finds this Governance Action to be constitutional. Our review concerns the constitutionality of the Info Action, which serves as a poll of the DReps regarding the Cardano Foundation’s initiative to apply for generic Top-Level Domains (gTLDs), not the commercial or strategic merits of the gTLD application itself. Constitutional Committee members may not prevent Info Actions from being recorded on-chain but they are allowed to express their views on whether the actions proposed within an Info Action would be constitutional if implemented by on-chain mechanisms (Article VII, Section 4).

This action was submitted by the Cardano Foundation to assess community sentiment regarding the registration of .ada and .cardano domains. Because the proposal explicitly states that the initiative will utilize the Foundation’s own financial resources and does not request funds from the Cardano Treasury, it does not trigger the substantive requirements or guardrails associated with Cardano Blockchain Ecosystem Budgets (Article IV and TREASURY-04a). Using an Info Action to gauge sentiment on an off-chain initiative without any future on-chain change is a valid exercise of governance rights within the decentralized ecosystem (Article III, Section 4).

[Precedent Discussion]
While this Info Action is constitutional, we reserve the right to evaluate the constitutionality of Info Actions based on proposed outcomes. An Info Action may be found unconstitutional if it seeks to direct or implement outcomes that are reserved for other governance action types, such as Treasury Withdrawals, Parameter Changes, or Updates to the Constitution, or if it attempts to commit funds, modify governance structures, or otherwise bypass the formal processes defined in the Cardano Constitution. Likewise, an Info Action may be unconstitutional if it proposes or encourages behavior that would violate the Constitution or its Tenets.

[Counterargument Discussion]
None

[Conclusion]
Due to its adherence to constitutional provisions under Articles III and VII of the Cardano Constitution, this Info Action is Constitutional.

[Internal Vote]
{"constitutional":5,"unconstitutional":0,"abstain":0,"didNotVote":0,"againstVote":0}

zwt49epsdedw__3285b7fd0da16d21e0b8f8910c.text:
[Summary]
Ace Alliance finds the proposed “Reimburse Ikigai Info Governance Action Deposit.” Governance Action **Constitutional**.

[Rationale Statement]
A [PDF version][pdf-link] of this rationale is also made available.

[pdf-link]: https://ipfs.io/ipfs/Qmd9KM2ooAxXef6Cw15Wq5xqTBW9wtUNV5dGV8LDZfq5fL

This Budget Info Action (gov\_action1x2zm0lgd59kjrc9clzgscdlh0ct62l8llr638h6t4a5jj4yqzzyqq2dgc7q) seeks a total of 103,000 ada to reimburse (100,000 ada) and compensate (3,000 ada) those who paid, but were not refunded, the 100,000 ada Governance Action deposit for Info Action “Cardanoの生きがい \- Ikigai \-” (gov\_action1t87n2vjnavthuggyarerafxx8c7n9mu4c7r96qlfp5uggsjdc8dsqymg588).

While this Budget Info Action indicates the 103,000 ada will be immediately distributed to the intended recipient, it does not signal intent to utilize the original Ikigai Action’s reward address (stake1uys93fhep4lc2u6lu0q09kcxayxzthasded35c0x0w60ugc9s0cm5), or any other specific reward address, as recipient to achieve this aim. Our decision finding this Action Constitutional is predicated on the direct distribution of such funds from the Treasury to the intended recipients. The link between the intended recipients and the recipient address to be designated in a future Treasury Withdrawal Governance Action must be substantiated by sufficient evidence in immutable form. While the clearest path to establishing this is designating the original Ikigai Action’s reward address as recipient of the funds, the fact said address remains unregistered risks further loss of funds unless registered.

Article III, Section 5 indicates “...all proposed governance actions are expected to follow a standardized and legible format including a URL and hash of all documented off-chain content to the Cardano Blockchain. Sufficient rationale shall be provided to justify the requested change to the Cardano Blockchain. The rationale shall include, at a minimum, a title, abstract, reason for the proposal, and relevant supporting materials. The content of every on-chain governance action must be identical to the final off-chain version of the proposed action.” This Budget Info Action meets these expectations. 

Article IV, Section 1 provides that “...budgets are expected to cover not less than a period of 73 epochs (approximately one calendar year) but nothing shall prevent the Cardano Community from proposing budgets for shorter or longer time periods.” This Budget Info Action, for the unique reasons cited, proposes a direct and immediate distribution of 103,000 ada to the specified recipient following approval of this Action and a subsequent Treasury Withdrawal Governance Action. 

Article IV, Section 2 requires “Cardano Blockchain budgets shall specify a process for overseeing use of funds from Cardano Blockchain treasury withdrawals including designating one or more administrators who shall be responsible for such oversight.” This Budget Info Action anticipates a direct and immediate distribution to the intended recipients upon approval of a subsequent Treasury Withdrawal Governance Action and names DReps as Administrator.  In this case, the process for overseeing the use of such funds both begins and ends with the same automated disbursement by the protocol from the Treasury to the designated recipient. Here, the protocol itself would function as Administrator for the purposes of compliance with this Section; overseeing this unique, unconditional, direct payment. Under these circumstances, such arrangement is objectively superior in transparency and efficacy than alternative options involving the introduction of an intermediary as Administrator — and an example of where possible and beneficial to “use blockchain technology in the governance process” (Preamble), and the utilization of “smart contracts and other other blockchain based tools to…ensure transparency” in the administration of this budget (Article IV, Section 2). While we appreciate the good faith intent of this Action’s proposers in naming DReps as Administrator for the purpose of compliance with this Section, we find the protocol itself satisfies such requirements here. Recognizing DReps as an Administrator poses unexplored potential risks, raises likely conflicts of interest, raises questions as to consent, and does not need to be explored or relied upon here. 

Article IV, Section 3, Sentence 1 provides “Withdrawals from the Cardano Blockchain treasury that would cause the Cardano Blockchain treasury balance to violate the then applicable net change limit shall not be permitted.” As indicated in our voting rationale for Budget Info Action “Stablecoin DeFi Liquidity Budget” (gov\_action1u4jrcvlkppjzuv5j9z5ksacwtvv77h6glu0knpcjut8gvjjfu0cqqt3alsy), “we cannot assess a Budget Info Action today, against an undetermined Net Change Limit in force on some future day, when a corresponding Treasury Withdrawal Governance Action might be evaluated. We have a Net Change Limit, and no Treasury Change results from either possible outcome of a proposed Budget Info Action. For these reasons, we reserve evaluation of constitutionality on Net Change Limit grounds for Treasury Withdrawal Governance Actions.”

Article IV, Section 4, Sentence 1 provides “Any governance action requesting ada from the Cardano Blockchain treasury shall require an allocation of ada as a part of such funding request to cover the cost of periodic independent audits and the implementation of oversight metrics as to the use of such ada.” The express purpose, unique facts, and optimally trustless proposed means underpinning this Budget Info Action fulfill the constitutional requirements of the Section. As laid out above in relation to Article IV, Section 2, the facts underlying this Action are distinguished from each of the ten preceding Budget Info Actions submitted. This Action endeavors to authorize a single automated transfer, for a uniquely remedial purpose, utilizing the most trustless and efficient means available. The alternative means — adding unnecessary intermediaries for the sake of transparency, audit, and oversight — would provide no additional benefit over protocol adjudication, and would only introduce intermediary risks cutting against those exact aims of the Constitution.

Article IV, Section 4, Sentence 2 indicates “Contractual obligations governing the use of ada received from the Cardano Blockchain treasury pursuant to a Cardano Blockchain ecosystem budget shall include dispute resolution provisions.” Based on the same context and reasoning detailed above, the protocol-adjudicated means proposed in this Action obviates the need for, and any benefit of, contractual obligations.

[Precedent Discussion]
This Budget Info Action is readily distinguished from the ten preceding actions of this type, including the two prior Budget Info Actions which Constitutional Committee Members found unconstitutional on grounds of Article IV, Section 2 and Article IV, Section 4, Sentence 1\. 

In both such Actions, “2025 Cardano Blockchain Ecosystem Budget \- 7.5M ₳ for community builders” (gov\_action1mmalzkcxpyn33t05hml2hxpwq0ffv6uu4mhe8sv7guz5nmm4afysqlmepzl) and “Tempo for Cardono Governance \- Maintenance & Development Budget for 2025” (gov\_action1f9da8n2yaf4updxzmwc7fnw8ug2gwnpvqays5n3yw6nz0mu3z2qqq6ggge9), the Actions sought ada from the Treasury to facilitate prospective work by the involved projects. The inherent risks of finally transferring funds directly to projects for prospective work is as objectively apparent to the Cardano Community today as it was to the Community members who drafted, passed, and ratified this Constitution. Accordingly, to mitigate such risk, the Constitution provides safeguards including the oversight and Administrator provision of Article IV, Section 2 and audit allocation provision of Article IV, Section 4\. In both Budget Info Actions found unconstitutional on these grounds, the application of these provisions pertained to the recognized risks associated with funding prospective work. For Actions with these characteristics, the provisions decentralize required off-chain trust, provide the precursors for traditional legal contracts and the remedies they enable, and bolster transparency and accountability. 

This budget Info Action, in contrast to the ten preceding it, is submitted for a remedial purpose, expressly intended to be finally and unconditionally settled (without provision for recourse in the event of future action or inaction by the recipient), and is most efficiently, trustlessly, and transparently achieved without the introduction of intermediaries to mitigate risk that is simply not present here.

[Counterargument Discussion]
None

[Conclusion]
Ace Alliance finds the proposed “Reimburse Ikigai Info Governance Action Deposit.” Governance Action **Constitutional**.

[Internal Vote]
{"constitutional":5,"unconstitutional":0,"abstain":0,"didNotVote":0,"againstVote":0}

zwt49epsdedw__03f671791fd97011f30e4d6b76.text:
[Summary]
We abstain on the “Loan ₳5,000,000 to Expand Cardano’s Global Listings” TWGA because of direct involvement in shaping the off-chain loan contract between Snek Foundation and Intersect.

[Rationale Statement]
A [PDF version][pdf-link] of this rationale is also made available.

[pdf-link]: https://ipfs.io/ipfs/QmaH9sTp68R6c1s7rmM55MovMwvB66R7rpxwuB7D9U6XXQ

Ace Alliance is abstaining on the “Loan ₳5,000,000 to Expand Cardano’s Global Listings” Treasury Withdrawal Governance Action because members of our consortium have been directly involved in shaping the off-chain loan contract between the Snek Foundation and Intersect.

One of the largest constitutional questions raised around this governance action pertains to whether the final loan agreement properly implements the terms described in the on-chain Budget Info Action (gov\_action1jr84r96lnsvu9yd6c0jhxe9gj5r7vnd2pgkntc6klplxdpyzz4tqqc9uldx)  and Treasury Withdrawal, and whether it satisfies requirements such as:

* The identity between on-chain content and final off-chain implementation (Article III, Section 5); and

* The requirement that contractual obligations govern the use of ada received from the Treasury under the approved budget (Article IV, Section 4).

Those questions are not abstract. They turn on the specific language, structure, and enforcement mechanics of the very contract that several Ace Alliance members have helped discuss, review, or shape in collaboration with the Snek Foundation, Intersect and other Cardano institutions.

In that context, we do not believe it is appropriate for Ace Alliance to:

* Participate deeply in crafting or refining the legal instrument, and then

* Turn around and present ourselves as an independent constitutional reviewer of that same instrument.

Even if we could, in principle, draw a sharp internal line between our “participant” role and our “reviewer” role, we are concerned that this would undermine the perceived integrity of the constitutional process. The Cardano community should not have to guess where our institutional involvement ends and our constitutional judgment begins when they are, in this particular case, so tightly intertwined.

[Precedent Discussion]
Cardano governance does anticipate that many of the people and organizations most qualified to contribute to governance actions will also be involved in designing, funding, or executing them. There are governance actions where this kind of involvement is normal and expected, such as:

* Budgets or Treasury Withdrawals that fund the Constitutional Committee or broader governance infrastructure;

* Ecosystem budgets that fund tools or services developed by organizations where governance participants are active.

In those cases, a CC member or DRep can often participate in both the design and the constitutional evaluation of an action, as long as the conflict is clear, properly disclosed, and the constitutional criteria are reasonably straightforward.

However, the nature of involvement in this particular governance action is different. We must consider the core constitutional question of whether the off-chain loan contract truly reflects and enforces the on-chain Budget Info Action and Treasury Withdrawal. 

We therefore see this abstention as a narrow, fact-specific precedent:

* It does *not* say that any involvement in a governance action automatically requires abstention.

* It *does* say that when a significant number of Ace Alliance members have been substantively involved in crafting the exact legal instrument whose conformity to the Constitution is being judged, we should err on the side of recusal.

This is the situation we face with the Snek–Intersect loan contract, and it is why we abstain here even though we might not abstain in other governance actions that also touch organizations we are part of.

[Counterargument Discussion]
None

[Conclusion]
Ace Alliance will abstain on the “Loan ₳5,000,000 to Expand Cardano’s Global Listings” Treasury Withdrawal Governance Action because our members have been substantively involved in shaping the final loan contract between the Snek Foundation and Intersect, and we do not consider it appropriate to conduct the constitutional analysis of a legal instrument we had influence over. Our abstention is a procedural decision rooted in conflict-of-interest and process integrity, and should not be interpreted as either support for or opposition to the constitutional merits of the governance action itself.

[Internal Vote]
{"constitutional":0,"unconstitutional":1,"abstain":4,"didNotVote":0,"againstVote":0}

zwt49epsdedw__d2745225498d1c56c0f01be997.text:
[Summary]
Ace Alliance finds the proposed "Constitutional Committee Compensation Epochs 581-653" Governance Action constitutional.

[Rationale Statement]
A [PDF version][pdf-link] of this rationale is also made available.

[pdf-link]: https://ipfs.io/ipfs/QmR7ujefNVuDg3skYrBHPWsxkvNze181kXJjEzKTewz32X

This Cardano Blockchain Ecosystem Budget Info Action (`gov_action16f69yf2f35w9ds8sr05ewyr55jg5f439mu8882rv295fvf8m4kcqql7y6nc`) seeks a budget of 1,000,000 ada to provide for the compensation and administrative costs of five sitting Constitutional Committee (CC) members for a 73-epoch term (Epochs 581-653).

**Article VII, Section 8**, states that "Cardano Blockchain ecosystem budgets shall provide for periodic administrative costs of the Constitutional Committee... as requested from time to time... and as approved by ada owners." This is further supported by **Article I, Section 1, Tenet 4**, which acknowledges "potential compensation to... CC members" as a means of fairly recognizing contributions.

The action adheres to all procedural and substantive requirements for a Budget Info Action:

1. **Procedural Requirements (Article III, Sec 5):** The proposal is presented in a standardized format with sufficient rationale and verifiable, immutable off-chain content, meeting all formal requirements.  
2. **Budget Purpose (Article IV, Sec 1):** The budget covers "governance process costs" for a "specific time period" of 73 epochs, aligning with constitutional requirements.  
3. **Administration & Oversight (Article IV, Sec 2):** The proposal designates a smart contract as the administrator and defines a robust oversight process. Each member's contract is auditable and overseen by the other four participating members, who collectively have the authority to pause further withdrawals if the designated member is no longer serving in their CC role. This peer-review mechanism satisfies the constitutional requirement for oversight.

**Conflict of Interest Disclosure**

Ace Alliance is a named beneficiary in this Cardano Blockchain Ecosystem Budget Info Action. Our review of this governance action is limited to assessing its constitutionality, and DReps assess the merits.

[Precedent Discussion]
None

[Counterargument Discussion]
None

[Conclusion]
Due to its direct constitutional mandate under Article VII and its full adherence to the procedural and substantive requirements of Articles III and IV, this Cardano Blockchain Ecosystem Budget Info Action is Constitutional.

[Internal Vote]
{"constitutional":5,"unconstitutional":0,"abstain":0,"didNotVote":0,"againstVote":0}

zwt49epsdedw__c882f194684d672316212f01ef.text:
[Summary]
Ace Alliance finds the Cardano Constitution v2.3 proposal to be **constitutional**.

[Rationale Statement]
A [PDF version][pdf-link] of this rationale is also made available.

[pdf-link]: https://ipfs.io/ipfs/QmYwhkNRgCMeXLmyFvNv6bnLk63u9oe8kJuo9jTDRiMUer

The proposed Cardano Blockchain Ecosystem Constitution v2.3 complies with the amendment requirements of Article VIII. It satisfies the documentation standards of Article III, Section 5, by providing immutable IPFS references, indicating differences from previous versions, and including supporting rationale.

This governance action seeks to reinforce the existing balance of powers between DReps, SPOs, and the Constitutional Committee without altering the framework established in the current Constitution.

[Precedent Discussion]
According to Article VII, Section 1, the Constitutional Committee must assess the constitutionality of all Governance Actions, including New Constitution actions. Questions have been raised as to whether the Constitutional Committee should evaluate procedural, but not substantive aspects of New Constitution actions, citing the possibility that this body could block the collective will of ada holders.

Cardano is governed through a constitutional system where the values and requirements delineated in the Constitution are the foundational expression of the will of ada holders, immutably recorded on the blockchain. However, if the Constitutional Committee should block a New Constitution, the DReps selected by ada holders may pass an Update Committee action to replace members of the Constitutional Committee with new members who interpret the constitution in line with their current collective will.

This system enforces checks and balances between the governance bodies, where the eagerness of DReps to adopt a Constitution with new rules and possibly new values is tempered by the Constitutional Committee’s duty to evaluate such actions. When reviewing this or any future New Constitution action, we must determine whether we are willing to risk our tenure on the Constitutional Committee in order to stand for the position that it is unconstitutional.

[Counterargument Discussion]
Some may raise concerns that updates to the Guardrails Appendix could alter treasury or network behavior in conflict with the written text of the Constitution. Independent review confirms that none of the amended guardrails are enforced by the on chain Guardrails Script. The change maintains both constitutional and operational continuity.

[Conclusion]
The Cardano Constitution v2.3 proposal is constitutional, procedurally correct, and provides a consistent text for community consideration.

[Internal Vote]
{"constitutional":5,"unconstitutional":0,"abstain":0,"didNotVote":0,"againstVote":0}

zwt49epsdedw__d2db60c5307cb517c735e2d013.text:
[Summary]
Ace Alliance finds the proposed “Withdraw ₳1,150,000 for GovTool 12 months active maintenance and development” Governance Action **Constitutional**.

[Rationale Statement]
A [PDF version][pdf-link] of this rationale is also made available.

[pdf-link]: https://ipfs.io/ipfs/QmXLbqYGZfG7HcKbVBTWN2mEdXtiAcyaeCg1Ny7AdoLY26

This Treasury Withdrawal Governance Action (gov\_action16tdkp3fs0j6303e4utgp8rftdug0ckezr4sslgv8wxdaeq40ngpsq5sr06h) seeks to withdrawal 1,150,000 ada from the Cardano Treasury for 12 months of GovTool maintenance and development.

Article III, Section 5 indicates “...all proposed governance actions are expected to follow a standardized and legible format including a URL and hash of all documented off-chain content to the Cardano Blockchain. Sufficient rationale shall be provided to justify the requested change to the Cardano Blockchain. The rationale shall include, at a minimum, a title, abstract, reason for the proposal, and relevant supporting materials. The content of every on-chain governance action must be identical to the final off-chain version of the proposed action.” This proposed Treasury Withdrawal Governance Action meets these expectations. 

Article IV, Section 3, Sentence 1 provides “Withdrawals from the Cardano Blockchain treasury that would cause the Cardano Blockchain treasury balance to violate the then applicable net change limit shall not be permitted.” This proposal, if enacted, would not violate the in-effect net change limit (gov\_action1nd3t833j7v5sz65k3tp9yyvztw60sjcjgcgjr37682s3m7frwrusqmd2k80). 

Article IV, Section 3, Sentence 2 provides “No withdrawals from the Cardano Blockchain treasury shall be permitted unless such withdrawals have been authorized and are being made pursuant to a budget for the Cardano Blockchain that is then in effect as required by the Cardano Blockchain Guardrails Appendix, and which has not been determined by the Constitutional Committee to be unconstitutional.” This Treasury Withdrawal Governance Action is submitted pursuant to the approved “Cardano GovTool Budget \- 12 months full active maintenance and development” Budget Info Action (gov\_action1n5sn54mgf47a7men2ryq6ppx88kta4wvenz2qkl4f9v6ppje8easqxwm88m).

Article IV, Section 4, Sentence 1 provides “Any governance action requesting ada from the Cardano Blockchain treasury shall require an allocation of ada as a part of such funding request to cover the cost of periodic independent audits and the implementation of oversight metrics as to the use of such ada.” In our assessment of governance action constitutionality, we consider information included in the proposed governance action and the linked, immutable content it incorporates. 

Here, the immutable proposed Treasury Withdrawal Governance Action rationale links to and incorporates the corresponding immutable Budget Info Action (gov\_action1n5sn54mgf47a7men2ryq6ppx88kta4wvenz2qkl4f9v6ppje8easqxwm88m) in the Abstract and Supporting Links sections. Accordingly, we consider this information in assessing constitutionality under Article IV, Section 4, Sentence 1\. 

Under the Constitutionality Checklist section of that Budget Info Action, and citing the relevant authority, it states “This proposal, if approved, intends to use part of the ₳100k committed by Intersect to cover cost of periodic independent audits.” Because we consider this linked, immutable information in our assessment, the only remaining question as to constitutionality on this point is whether this satisfies Article IV, Section 4, Sentence 1 by constituting “an allocation of ada as a part of such funding request.” 

We find that it does satisfy this requirement for two reasons. 

First, this constitutes an allocation of ada — it allocates ₳100k in the possession of the Administrator for this purpose — and that allocation is a part of such funding request, the Treasury Withdrawal Governance Action and incorporated Budget Info Action. The Constitution does not require, for example, “an allocation of ada as a part of such \*funding received\*,” or “an allocation of ada \*from\* such requested funds.” Here, the text of the incorporated Budget Info Action constitutes an allocation of ₳100k as a part of such funding request. 

Second, finding an action unconstitutional for proactively allocating Administrator-held ada to cover these costs conflicts with the objective purpose of the requirement — this proposal’s provision for the cost of audit and oversight is more restrictive than what the Constitution requires, not less.   

Article IV, Section 4, Sentence 2 provides “Contractual obligations governing the use of ada received from the Cardano Blockchain treasury pursuant to a Cardano Blockchain ecosystem budget shall include dispute resolution provisions.” The proposal indicates Intersect-facilitated legal contracts will be entered into which include dispute resolution processes. 

Article IV, Section 5 requires “Any ada received from a Cardano Blockchain treasury withdrawal, so long as such ada is being held directly or indirectly by an administrator prior to further disbursement, must be kept in one or more separate accounts that can be audited by the Cardano Community, and such accounts shall not be delegated to an SPO but must be delegated to the predefined auto abstain voting option.” This proposal fulfills the constitutional requirement, as Intersect’s Treasury Reserve Smart Contract (TRSC) is the designated recipient of the requested funds, and the provided stake credential is not delegated to a stake pool and delegated to the auto-abstain pre-defined voting option.

[Precedent Discussion]
In this decision we specify: 

1.) In our assessment of governance action constitutionality, we consider information included in the proposed governance action and the linked, immutable content it incorporates. While we find the specific facts underpinning this Treasury Withdrawal Governance Action did not violate Article IV, Section 4, Sentence 1, we strongly advise future drafters of Treasury Withdrawal Governance Action to directly provide for Article IV, Section 4, Sentence 1 compliance instead of relying on Budget Info Action incorporation.

2.) In our assessment of Article IV, Section 4, Sentence 1, we decline to narrowly interpret “...an allocation of ada as a part of such funding request…” in a manner counter to its objective purpose — to ensure that ada is allocated for the cost of periodic independent audits and the implementation of oversight metrics. Here, the Treasury Withdrawal Governance Action’s allocation of ada for this purpose is currently held by the Administrator, Intersect. While finding the action unconstitutional on this basis would be counter to the provision’s purpose under these specific facts, that same analysis for other facts presented in a future Treasury Withdrawal Governance Action could result in the opposite conclusion. Accordingly, we strongly advise that future Treasury Withdrawal Governance Action drafters specify an allocation of ada from the amount to be disbursed following successful action enactment to mitigate the other possible outcome of this fact-dependent analysis.

[Counterargument Discussion]
We decline to find this Treasury Withdrawal Governance Action unconstitutional on the basis of Article IV, Section 4, Sentence 1, for the reasons provided in the above Rationale Statement and Precedent Discussion sections.

[Conclusion]
Ace Alliance finds the proposed “Withdraw ₳1,150,000 for GovTool 12 months active maintenance and development” Governance Action **Constitutional**.

[Internal Vote]
{"constitutional":4,"unconstitutional":0,"abstain":1,"didNotVote":0,"againstVote":0}

zwt49epsdedw__996edfe370b8e51be73541e754.text:
[Summary]
The Ace Alliance finds this Info Action constitutional. We comment only on its constitutionality, not merits of referenced materials, in line with Article VII, Section 4 of the Cardano Constitution.

[Rationale Statement]
A [PDF version][pdf-link] of this rationale is also made available.

[pdf-link]: https://ipfs.io/ipfs/QmRAYYWbVAinoGMio28XBD7mdYwhyu9wWpuStC83PSh1o1

The Ace Alliance finds this Governance Action to be constitutional. Our review concerns the constitutionality of the Info Action, which is a poll of the DReps, not the merits of the Cardano Product Committee’s draft “Cardano 2030 Vision & Strategy” document. Constitutional Committee members may not prevent Info Actions from being recorded on chain but they are allowed to express their views on whether the actions proposed within an Info Action would be constitutional if implemented by on-chain mechanisms (Article VII, Section 4).
This poll of the DReps was submitted by an anonymous community member, not the Cardano Product Committee of Intersect who are authoring the “Vision & Strategy” document in question. It requests DReps to express opinions on a version of the Cardano Product Committee’s draft plan. Because Cardano is a decentralized ecosystem, any community participant may post such a poll to gauge sentiment in this way. We also note that the referenced document may have changed between the time this Info Action was submitted and the current version of the Cardano Product Committee’s materials. However, this evolution does not affect the constitutionality of the action, since Info Actions may reference ongoing or partial work without violating constitutional principles.

[Precedent Discussion]
While this Info Action is constitutional, we reserve the right to evaluate the constitutionality of Info Actions based on proposed outcomes. An Info Action may be found unconstitutional if it seeks to direct or implement outcomes that are reserved for other governance action types, such as Treasury Withdrawals, Parameter Changes, or Updates to the Constitution, or if it attempts to commit funds, modify governance structures, or otherwise bypass the formal processes defined in the Cardano Constitution. Likewise, an Info Action may be unconstitutional if it proposes or encourages behavior that would violate the Constitution or its Tenets

[Counterargument Discussion]
None

[Conclusion]
Due to its adherence to constitutional provisions under Article VII of the Cardano Constitution, this Info Action is Constitutional.

[Summary]
Ace Alliance finds the proposed “Stablecoin DeFi Liquidity Budget” Constitutional.

[Rationale Statement]
A [PDF version][pdf-link] of this rationale is also made available.

[pdf-link]: https://ipfs.io/ipfs/QmSZxNCN9U9W2CY3oeyEpnnCHebC5yUNUeh7BzU2FUkDf1

This Budget Info Action (gov\_action1u4jrcvlkppjzuv5j9z5ksacwtvv77h6glu0knpcjut8gvjjfu0cqqt3alsy) seeks 50 Million ada (50,000,000) to increase stablecoin liquidity through the creation of a fund for deployment into decentralized finance (DeFi) protocols. Fund assets would consist only of ada, fiat-backed stablecoins, and DeFi protocol liquidity tokens, and a nine-person committee would administer funds utilizing smart contracts. An overseeing treasury DAO would have the ability to impeach or elect committee members, and may wind down the fund to return the assets to the Cardano Treasury.   

The proposed governance action does not violate relevant procedural requirements. It includes title, abstract, reason, supporting materials, and utilizes hashing for off-chain references (Art. III, Sections 4-5). It would contribute to developing the ecosystem (Art. IV, Section 1), and specifies a sufficient budget duration of 73 epochs, approximately one (1) year, for corresponding treasury withdrawal(s) (Art. IV, Section 2). As explained in the Precedent Discussion section below, we reserve evaluation of constitutionality on Net Change Limit grounds for Treasury Withdrawal Governance Actions. The proposal would facilitate transparent on-chain audit, integrates accountability, and commits to delegate auto-abstain and refrain from SPO delegation (Art. IV, Sections 4-5).

[Precedent Discussion]
The Constitution's Net Change Limit requirements apply exclusively to Treasury Withdrawal Governance Actions (Article IV, Section 3 and guardrails TREASURY-01a through TREASURY-04a). Moreover, we cannot assess a Budget Info Action today, against an undetermined Net Change Limit in force on some future day, when a corresponding Treasury Withdrawal Governance Action might be evaluated. We have a Net Change Limit, and no Treasury Change results from either possible outcome of a proposed Budget Info Action. For these reasons, we reserve evaluation of constitutionality on Net Change Limit grounds for Treasury Withdrawal Governance Actions.

[Counterargument Discussion]
None

[Conclusion]
Due to its adherence to constitutional provisions under Articles III and IV, this Budget Info Action is Constitutional.

zwwv8uu8vgl5__c21b00f90f18fce4003edf42b0.text:
[Summary]
We consider this governance action to be constitutional.

[Rationale Statement]
This proposal is a governance action concerning a protocol parameter change that adjusts the per-transaction and per-block memory execution limits for Plutus scripts. The proposal satisfies the relevant guardrails set forth in the amended Cardano Constitution, including those relating to maximum limits, the magnitude of change, voting requirements, advance notice periods, and verification requirements. No clear conflict with the constitutional principles or provisions has been identified. For these reasons, the proposal is determined to be consistent with the Constitution.

[Precedent Discussion]
None

[Counterargument Discussion]
However, as a supplementary opinion, the following view was expressed. While the main off-chain document supporting this governance action is immutable through the use of an IPFS CID, the core technical justification presented in the rationale—including performance evaluation, benchmarking evidence, and records of technical approval—relies on external web resources that are mutable and may be altered or become unavailable after submission. Article II, Section 6 of the amended Cardano Constitution requires that documents providing the additional context necessary to evaluate a governance action be immutable and verifiable after submission. From this perspective, reliance on mutable external references may undermine the long-term verifiability of key technical claims contained in the proposal. Accordingly, while the formal requirements of the Article are satisfied, this view holds that stricter standards for ensuring the immutability of substantively supporting materials would more closely align with the underlying intent of the Constitution, particularly with respect to verifiability and accountability.

[Conclusion]
For the reasons stated above, we determine that it is constitutional.

zwwv8uu8vgl5__4b10e5793208cb8f228756e021.text:
[Summary]
We consider this governance action to be unconstitutional.

[Rationale Statement]
This proposal is determined to be unconstitutional, as it fails to satisfy multiple requirements of the amended Cardano Constitution. First, the off-chain documentation associated with this governance action is hosted on a mutable GitHub URL, such that the contents of the referenced documents remain technically alterable even after submission. However, Article II, Section 6 of the Cardano Constitution explicitly requires that “any document hosted at the referenced URL must be immutable.” Because the proposal relies on documentation that does not meet this immutability requirement at the time of submission, the proposal fails to ensure the verifiability and integrity of the governance action as required by the Constitution. Second, the proposal asserts that the requested Treasury Withdrawal is based on an “approved budget,” citing a previously ratified Stablecoin DeFi Liquidity Budget via a Budget Info Action. Under the amended Constitution, the Budget Info Action has been abolished, and Info Actions no longer possess any legal or constitutional authority to approve budgets. Accordingly, the cited Budget Info Action cannot constitute a valid constitutional basis for an approved budget, and the prerequisite conditions for a Treasury Withdrawal are therefore not met. For these reasons, this proposal does not comply with the amended Cardano Constitution and is thus determined to be unconstitutional.

[Precedent Discussion]
None

[Counterargument Discussion]
None

[Conclusion]
For the reasons stated above, we determine that it is unconstitutional.

We consider this governance action to be constitutional.

[Rationale Statement]
This proposal is an Information (Info) Action intended to share the Cardano 2030 Vision, Mission, Strategy Framework, and KPIs with the community. It does not entail any protocol changes, constitutional amendments, treasury withdrawals, nor does it impose any legally binding effect on future voting behavior. The proposal satisfies the submission requirements set forth in Article III, Section 5 of the Cardano Constitution, and is therefore considered constitutional.

[Precedent Discussion]
None

[Counterargument Discussion]
The achievement of the proposed KPIs will critically depend on future governance and budget processes. Without clearly identifying which entities are responsible for committing to these KPIs, and without aligning treasury allocations accordingly in subsequent annual budgets, there is a significant risk that these targets will remain aspirational rather than operational, and their practical realization may be materially undermined.

[Conclusion]
For the reasons stated above, we determine that it is constitutional.

zwwv8uu8vgl5__91a79f5c934b7c91e3027736d5.text:
[Summary]
We consider this governance action to be constitutional.

[Rationale Statement]
This proposal operates within the framework of the existing constitutional principles and guardrails. It removes non-binding and advisory provisions, as well as redundant mechanisms such as the Budget Info Action, while clarifying definitions, ensuring the immutability of proposal documents, and applying audit and accountability requirements uniformly to treasury withdrawals. The objective of these changes is to simplify governance and strengthen transparency and accountability. These amendments do not infringe upon the separation of powers among DReps, SPOs, and the Constitutional Committee, nor do they alter voting thresholds or undermine constraints imposed by constitutional guardrails. Furthermore, the proposal has been submitted as a formal constitutional update in accordance with the prescribed amendment procedures. Based on the above, we determine that this governance action is constitutional under the spirit and provisions of the Cardano Constitution.

[Precedent Discussion]
None

[Counterargument Discussion]
As this governance action establishes a constitutional text on-chain, it would have been preferable for the proposal to include, in addition to the reasons for addressing existing issues, a clearer explanation of why the revised wording should be regarded as sufficient as a final constitutional document. Furthermore, given the possibility that this proposal represents the first constitutional amendment, it is noted that there is currently no shared convention governing the treatment of constitutional version numbering. While neither of these points undermines the constitutionality of the proposal, greater clarity and explanation would be desirable in future constitutional amendments in order to enhance the stability, clarity, and public legitimacy of the Constitution, as well as to support the development of clearer operational conventions.

[Conclusion]
For the reasons stated above, we determine that it is constitutional.

The proposal offers only a brief and high-level description of the budget, which may be considered limited in the context of a treasury withdrawal–related governance action. Article III, Section 5 of the Constitution states that governance actions are expected to provide sufficient rationale and supporting materials so that the community can make an informed decision. While the proposers explain that detailed cost information cannot be disclosed due to confidentiality obligations such as non-disclosure agreements, even within these constraints there may be room to provide additional information within the scope of permissible disclosure. In addition, the proposal does not clearly specify concrete objectives or measurable outcomes, which makes it more difficult to assess how the use of funds and the resulting outcomes would be evaluated or overseen. From the perspectives of transparency and accountability, these points suggest that the proposal merits careful consideration.

[Conclusion]

As such, it remains fully compliant with the Treasury Guardrails defined in Appendix I, Section 3 — particularly TREASURY-01a(x) and TREASURY-02a(x) — which govern the application and continuity of a previously established NCL. The extension ensures continued treasury operations beyond the original expiration date and preserves the spending ceiling agreed upon by the Cardano Community. Therefore, we deem this governance action to be constitutional.

None

Nonetheless, these concerns relate to procedural efficiency rather than constitutionality.

zwwv8uu8vgl5__3285b7fd0da16d21e0b8f8910c.text:
[Summary]
We abstain from a constitutional determination.

[Rationale Statement]
The claim for the outstanding portion of the deposit, 100,000 ADA, in this proposal is justified. However, the claim for 3,000 ADA can be considered unjustified. As opinions within the team were divided, we abstain from making a constitutional judgment.

[Precedent Discussion]
None

[Counterargument Discussion]
(Article I, Tenet 8 and Tenet 9) The redemption of the additional 3,000 ADA, which is not part of the deposit, is considered personal compensation and is therefore inappropriate. Furthermore, the following elements are insufficient for a treasury withdrawal proposal. (Article 3, Section 5) The explanation of the circumstances under which the 100,000 ADA cannot be recovered (issues with the relevant wallet or system) is not explicitly provided. (Article 4, Section 2) The proposal merely states that the administrator is “DRep,” without specifying who is responsible for what. (Article 4, Section 5) A community-auditable receiving address is not provided.

[Conclusion]
The request to withdraw 100,000 ADA itself is legitimate, but the 3,000 ADA portion cannot necessarily be deemed constitutional. Furthermore, the information provided is insufficient for a treasury withdrawal governance action. Therefore, we abstain.

[Summary]
We consider this governance action to be constitutional.

[Rationale Statement]
This determination was primarily based on Articles 3 and 8, both of which were judged to be constitutional. (Article 3, Section 5) Reasons were provided for numerous changes, and off-chain content URLs and supplementary materials were attached. Based on these, it was determined that there were no formal issues. (Article 8, Section 1) This provision encourages proposing amendments following discussion as necessary. The necessity of the amendments and the discussion process since the previous proposal were clearly documented, presenting no issues. Furthermore, the added definitions and the provisions of Article 2, Paragraphs 6 and 7 were accompanied by clear justifications, which also presented no issues.

[Precedent Discussion]
None

[Counterargument Discussion]
(Article 3, Section 5 “Sufficient rationale”) While we understand that many DRep opinions have been incorporated since the previous v2.0 proposal, as before, attempting to make numerous changes at once means not all changes are accompanied by stated reasons. Furthermore, the sheer number of changes combined with only brief summaries of the reasons means DReps and Constitution Committee members still cannot thoroughly examine each individual change. For example, there is no explanation for naming this version v2.3 despite it being the first constitutional amendment, nor for changing the preamble. Unless proposals focus on specific issues, such as preamble changes or definition additions, the result will be a constitutional amendment that fails to sufficiently reflect the careful deliberation of DReps and Constitution Committee members. Rather than such comprehensive amendments, proposing changes step by step, issue by issue, is more constructive. (Article 3, Section 5 “Legible format”) While the comparison document for changes highlights relevant sections with color coding, evaluators must still independently discern how the text has changed. This cannot be considered a legible format.

[Conclusion]
Upon review of the proposed amendments, we note that while certain concerns remain, no clear or decisive violations of the current Constitution were identified. Accordingly, the proposal is considered constitutional.

[Internal Vote]
{"constitutional":4,"unconstitutional":1,"abstain":0,"didNotVote":0}

There are no issues from a formal standpoint. (Article 4, Section 1) This proposal concerns a one-year governance process and does not deviate from this clause. (Article 4, Section 2) Budget management is delegated to Intersect, utilizing the Treasury Reserve Smart Contract (TRSC) and the Project-Specific Smart Contract (PSSC), which we deem acceptable. (Article 4, Section 5) The designated account is not delegated to the SPO, and DRep delegation is auto-abstain type, so it is acceptable.

[Precedent Discussion]
None

[Counterargument Discussion]
None

[Conclusion]
For the reasons stated above, we unanimously determined that it is constitutional.

ztwq6mh5jkgw__4b10e5793208cb8f228756e021.text:
[Summary]
Unfortunate timing for this proposal which arrives alongside a constitutional change. Few misalignments mostly due to this.

[Rationale Statement]
The main offending traits are:

- The main rationale document isn't content-addressed, and being a malleable URL as per Article II Section 6. Even though that constitutional requirement is a bit redundant due to the presence of a content hash alongside the URL, it is nonetheless required. 

- As per Article 2 Section 7.1, the terms of the withdrawal must also be clearly stated. Yet the period of delivery and refunding conditions aren't quite clear or stated. There are multiple timeframes mentioned in the proposal, but the overall timeline is unclear. 

Also, not as important but still encouraged: the proposal refers to its past budget without explicitly linking to it. To avoid confusion or misinterpretation, I'd advise to always clearly communicate a governance action id or at minima a transaction hash when referring to previous proposals.

ztwq6mh5jkgw__c21b00f90f18fce4003edf42b0.text:
[Summary]
LGTM.

[Rationale Statement]
The proposal follows from a long-running discussion and a formal protocol-parameter change to the relevant committee at Intersect made a while back (well beyond 3 months as required by the PARAM-06a guardrail).

Visible efforts have been made towards benchmarking the effect of the change and it has been confirmed to not disrupt the current network status. 

It is worth nothing, though, that while the proposal includes a reversion plan; the execution of that plan will *likely* be unconstitutional since it could prevent the execution of smart contracts and thus, permanently lock funds of Ada holders who would have interacted with impacted scripts. 

However, that fact is acknowledged in the proposal and voters are therefore assumed voting with full knowledge.

ztwq6mh5jkgw__a633504ddc47a852c4ae033c75.text:
[Summary]
Solid budget, albeit confusing in terms of deliveries.

[Rationale Statement]
The proposal is clearly positioned as a budget, makes provision for an external audit and has a clearly defined administrator over a multisig-guarded committee. 

The budget is well-articulated, and comes with relevant KPIs and rationale.

ztwq6mh5jkgw__91a79f5c934b7c91e3027736d5.text:
[Summary]
LGTM

[Rationale Statement]
As per Article VIII - Section 1, amendments to the constitution are allowed and the constitution shall be treated as a living document. This is exactly the road taken by the authors who have been repeatedly bringing constitution updates to the table while incorporating feedback from the community. 

I believe that the *content* of the new constitution is not subject to any review from the constitutional committee (CC); for the CC's role is to be the guardian of the constitution, whatever it is decided to be. A new constitution cannot -- by construction -- be unconstitutional. Only the process for establishing one could.

ztwq6mh5jkgw__f8393f1ff814d3d52336a97712.text:
[Summary]
👍

[Rationale Statement]
With epoch 603 over, the proposal is *now* (and only now...) indeed constitutional:

- As per Article IV. Section 3; any withdrawal must be made pursuant to an approved budget. While it is questionable that the budget is sufficiently detailed, it was positively met with an overwhelming majority. The same authors and a clear reference to that budget can be found with the proposal.

- As per its latest date-range extension ( gov_action169kllwhfmp488je5x5rwvufd08p8sztdcf0ghf5sp6ey2gnjdwaqql47xry ), the current Net Change-Limit is set to 350M Ada and covers the period from the start of epoch 532 until the end of epoch 612. 277,031,507 Ada have been withdrawn from the treasury over that period. A 70M withdrawals brings this to 347,031,507 Ada; which is ~3M below the current NCL. 

The proposal also covers for the usual points regarding funds administration, audits and delegations.

ztwq6mh5jkgw__8f54d021c6e6fcdd5a4908f10a.text:
[Summary]
-

[Rationale Statement]
A [PDF version][pdf-link] of this rationale is also made available.

[pdf-link]: https://ipfs.io/ipfs/QmSpzQAT86ZtHQC8s6kLFXwQ6Dh7mQdyEhYdwixZs7j6Dm

The proposal is hardly a *budget*, although itself presents it as such. Being an info action with no on-chain consequences, I have treated as what it claims to: a budget.

Under that lens, the proposal spans over 24-months, is self-administered by one of the proposer (Intersect), and provisions for a financial audit. However, I do not believe the rationale to be detailed and sufficient (in particular for a budget of that magnitude and from already well-funded actors that *definitely have the resources to do better*).

Hence, this constitutes a violation of Article III - Section 5 of the constitution. Such a violation is, however, entirely subjective. And I am well aware of my bias and my affiliation towards one of the author (CF). Thus, I cannot vote 'YES' on this proposal in good faith without my vote being interpreted as direct support to my employer when I deem the quality and level of details in the proposal clearly insufficient. 

For now, I'll therefore *abstain* on this proposal with the intention to follow through for the withdrawal unless the latter can address the gaps left in the budget.

ztwq6mh5jkgw__d16dffbae9d86a73cb343506e6.text:
[Summary]
The propose info action qualifies as a Net-Change Limit (NCL) extension and rightfully extends the previously established "2025 NCL" of 350M ADA by 8 epochs (thus ending at the end of Epoch 612).

[Rationale Statement]
There's no explicit constitutional duties for constitutional committee (CC) members regarding NCLs, nor is there any formal definition of what an NCL is. 

However, it is my interpretation that whether an info action qualifies as an NCL is a constitutional matter and thus, pertains to CC duties. 

While unusual, this info action can only be framed in the context of an NCL. Furthermore, the information provided is accurate.

ztwq6mh5jkgw__03f671791fd97011f30e4d6b76.text:
[Summary]
🐍

[Rationale Statement]
A [PDF version][pdf-link] of this rationale is also made available.

[pdf-link]: https://ipfs.io/ipfs/QmWFsRjy2tK78uGmLQJCq8ijvToZupFWzLDvtTg2yJia93

My original intention was to declare this proposal UNCONSTITUTIONAL due to a violation of Article IV - Section 1 which demands that withdrawal proposals "give effect to [..] budgets in effect". However, the current proposal presents a few discrepancies with its original budget:

- No contractual obligation to return the borrowed funds back to the treasury. The only highlighted obligation is to CDH, which is not Cardano's treasury.

- No 'continuous repayment' of the loan clearly defined but instead, only a vague commitment to propose a repayment schedule after 3 years.

- None of the shared documents / agreements is final, have dates or signatures from any of the involved parties.

So, in its current state, the withdrawal does not in fact give effect to its budget for it is changing the agreed upon rules and leave blanks in places that require clarity.

However, while unusual, IntersectMBO has shared (https://x.com/IntersectMBO/status/1991093096017514571) precisions on the current proposals addressing the points raised above, as well as a few others. In particular, the document states that IntersectMBO will "publish the final loan agreement [..] prior to any first disbursement from the Treasury Reserve Smart Contract to the Snek Foundation’s project-specific contract". This memo has been anchored on-chain at slot 171991447 under the transaction 78feee8bb9435f445f204d72e9d2ac827858c94863b2a34c41d60d7b337fc0ef.

I recognise that all this isn't quite conventional, but I firmly believe that the CC exists specifically for cases that aren't quite conventional (where an automated process would suffice).

Under these new terms, and all else being otherwise okay, the proposal thereby appears CONSTITUTIONAL.

[Summary]
The document is too ambiguous in its intent. It is half-way between a constitution and a budget proposal. Yet it is neither. Approving this document could help it being later weaponized to justify budgets and/or actions that do not go through due diligence.

[Rationale Statement]
A [PDF version][pdf-link] of this rationale is also made available.

[pdf-link]: https://ipfs.io/ipfs/QmaJhASVZv4AU25tHKSThfkZ9e5ULrjfwd7SmWZBaDEuJ3

The "Cardano 2030: Our Future, Defined" document is framed very much like a constitution, yet it is not presented as such. It overlaps with the constitution in many aspects; yet it does not undergo the same level of approval as a constitution amendment. In this, it violates Article VIII Section 2. 

Second, it could also be interpreted as laying the foundation of a budget proposal. Which is also dangerous as it then becomes a vehicle for fast-tracking under-specified budgets and poorly justified withdrawal requests on the sole basis that they are aligned with the Cardano 2030 vision. 

The lack of author (and accountability thereof) on the document adds also to the ambiguity and uncertainty around it. While the document is an interesting take coming straight from the Intersect Product Committee, it has been seemingly proposed without prior agreement with the Product Committee.

zwz2a08a8cqd__0b19476e40bbbb5e1e8ce15352.text:
[Summary]
We have determined that this Hard Fork Initiation governance action is constitutional.

[Rationale Statement]
Article III Section 6 Paragraph 3 of the Interim Constitution states "Hard Fork Initiation and Protocol Parameter Change governance actions should undergo sufficient technical review and scrutiny as mandated by the Cardano Blockchain Guardrails to ensure that the governance action does not endanger the security, functionality or performance of the Cardano Blockchain. Governance actions should address their expected impact on the Cardano Blockchain ecosystem."

The guardrails that require consideration for the governance action with ID "0b19476e40bbbb5e1e8ce153523762e2b6859e7ecacbaf06eae0ee6a447e79b9#0" and title "Hard Fork to Protocol Version 10 ("Plomin" Hard Fork)" are HARDFORK-01, HARDFORK-02, HARDFORK-03, HARDFORK-04, HARDFORK-05, HARDFORK-06, HARDFORK-07, HARDFORK-08 and INTERIM-01. These are addressed as follows:
* HARDFORK-01, HARDFORK-02 and HARDFORK-03 - The protocol version change being introduced sufficiently addresses these guardrails.
* HARDFORK-05, HARDFORK-06, HARDFORK-07 and HARDFORK-08 - There are no changes relevant to these guardrails.
* HARDFORK-04 - At the time this vote was submitted, more than 85% of stake pools by active stake have upgraded to support the new protocol version.
* INTERIM-01 - More than 18 epochs have elapsed since the Chang hard fork, fulfilling the quantitative requirements of this guardrail.

Further, the rationale supplied with this governance action provides adequate information regarding the technical reviews that were performed for this new version; including security, functionality and performance evaluations, as required by Article III Section 6 Paragraph 3 of the Interim Constitution.

By way of a split decision we find this governance action **Constitutional**.

[Precedent Discussion]
None

[Counterargument Discussion]
In the lead up to submitting this vote, we tracked several risks associated with both supporting the ratification of the upgrade (though a "Constitutional" vote), as well as supporting a rejection of the upgrade (through an "Unconstitutional" vote).

Our primary (unresolved) concern if the hard fork is ratified, relates to either insufficient DRep delegation or the concentration of DRep voting power. As INTERIM-01 states that its intent is to "...provide sufficient time for DReps to register and campaign and for Ada holders to choose their initial voting delegations...", it could be argued that the presence of this risk means there hasn not yet been sufficient time. Further, in reference to Article III Section 6 Paragraph 3, the consequence of this risk could be deemed to "endanger the security, functionality or performance of the Cardano Blockchain" if not properly managed.

These considerations were assessed against the impact to the broader ecosystem if the hard fork is rejected; such as the inability to ratify the draft Constitution, progress the budget process to finance important changes within the Cardano ecosystem, and the potential knock-on effects that any negative perceptions that may result could have on projects building applications, maintaining the infrastructure or participating in the ecosystem in any other way.

[Conclusion]
While acknowledging meaningful risks regarding DRep participation, we find that the governance action's strong technical compliance with constitutional requirements and guardrails outweighs these concerns. Based on our 5-1 vote, we deem this action constitutional, recognising that enabling the ecosystem's continued development through this hard fork serves the broader constitutional intent.

[Internal Vote]
{"constitutional":5,"unconstitutional":1,"abstain":0,"didNotVote":0}

zwz2a08a8cqd__b2a591ac219ce6dcca5847e024.text:
[Summary]
We have determined that this parameter change governance action is constitutional.

[Rationale Statement]
Article III Section 6 Paragraph 3 of the Interim Constitution states "Hard Fork Initiation and Protocol Parameter Change governance actions should undergo sufficient technical review and scrutiny as mandated by the Cardano Blockchain Guardrails to ensure that the governance action does not endanger the security, functionality or performance of the Cardano Blockchain. Governance actions should address their expected impact on the Cardano Blockchain ecosystem."

The guardrails that require consideration for the governance action with ID "b2a591ac219ce6dcca5847e0248015209c7cb0436aa6bd6863d0c1f152a60bc5#0" and title "Plutus V3 Cost Model Parameter Changes Prior to Chang#2" are PCM-01, PCM-02 and PCM-03.  The rationale supplied with this governance action sufficiently addresses the criteria in these guardrails.

Further, the rationale provides information regarding the technical review that was performed in relation to this change.  As the cost model parameter change is only enabled with the next hard fork and is something that needs to be optionally used by dapps, we are satisfied with the technical evaluation that was performed.

We therefore find this governance action **Constitutional**.

[Precedent Discussion]
None

[Counterargument Discussion]
None

[Conclusion]
This governance action sufficiently addresses the technical review and guardrails criteria of the Interim Constitution, so is deemed constitutional.

zvvcpkl3443y__gov_action1cgdsp7g0rr7wgqp.text:
[Summary]
Tingvard judges this protocol parameter update governance action constitutional.

[Rationale Statement]
This governance action is properly framed as a “Protocol Parameter Changes” action and concerns adjustments to critical protocol parameters governing Plutus script memory execution limits. The proposal increases maxTxExecutionUnits[memory] and maxBlockExecutionUnits[memory] in a correlated manner, consistent with the requirements for network and execution-budget parameters under the Constitution.
The action satisfies the procedural and substantive requirements of Article II, § 6. The proposal is presented in a standardized format, includes immutable off-chain metadata with a corresponding hash, and provides a comprehensive technical rationale, benchmarking evidence, and guardrail analysis. The metadata demonstrates that the proposed changes were preceded by extended off-chain discussion, formal consideration by the Intersect Parameter Committee, and subsequent ratification by the Technical Steering Committee, satisfying expectations of transparency and technical scrutiny.
The proposed parameter values remain within all mandatory guardrails set out in the Cardano Blockchain Guardrails Appendix. The increases do not exceed maximum bounds, do not reduce existing limits, and respect the recommended per-epoch change constraints. The proposal explicitly acknowledges the critical nature of the affected parameters and correctly relies on the additional SPO voting threshold required for such changes, in accordance with PARAM-03a.
From a constitutional perspective, the action advances the Tenets set out in Article I. In particular, it promotes Tenet 3 by ensuring that developers are not unreasonably prevented from deploying applications as intended, and Tenet 8 by enabling more efficient use of existing block capacity without wasteful fragmentation or artificial constraints. Real-world development experience has demonstrated that current memory limits force developers to choose between reducing supported functionality or abandoning features that are standard on competing platforms, even after extensive optimization and use of advanced tooling. This update removes such artificial barriers while remaining within safe and benchmarked performance limits.
The proposal also considers potential trade-offs. Increasing memory execution limits marginally raises hardware requirements for block-producing nodes. However, benchmarking confirms that Praos timing guarantees remain intact, and the resulting requirements remain well within the capacity of the vast majority of existing stake pool operators. The Constitution does not require preservation of minimum-spec operation where doing so would unnecessarily impede the evolution and competitiveness of the ecosystem.

[Precedent Discussion]
This decision affirms that incremental, benchmark-driven increases to Plutus execution limits are constitutionally permissible when they are supported by rigorous testing, comply with guardrails, and are introduced through a transparent, staged process. It reinforces the principle that protocol parameters may evolve to reflect real-world usage and developer needs, provided that security, performance, and decentralization requirements are respected.

[Counterargument Discussion]
A potential counterargument is that increasing execution limits may gradually raise the baseline hardware requirements for SPOs, potentially affecting decentralization. However, the Constitution does not mandate static operational requirements, and the evidence presented demonstrates that the change preserves network performance guarantees and remains within practical operational norms. No constitutional provision is violated by such an increase when it is justified, proportionate, and technically validated.

[Conclusion]
For these reasons, Tingvard finds this protocol parameter update governance action constitutional. It complies with Article II, § 6, adheres to all applicable Guardrails, respects the Cardano Blockchain Tenets, and represents a measured, evidence-based evolution of the protocol in support of long-term sustainability and developer usability.
